data.title: Birkenstock: Upgrade To Buy On Cheaper Valuation
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Birkenstock (NYSE:BIRK) stock previously with a hold rating, as I did not find the upside attractive at that valuation. I have now changed my mind. Valuation has become a lot cheaper after the sell-down. The stock is trading at ~22x forward P/E as I write this update, and since fundamentals have not deteriorated, I am upgrading to a buy.

Resilient Top-Line Growth Across Channels and Geographies

Like I have expected, BIRK continues to report robust revenue growth rates, showing no signs of momentum slowdown. For the benefit of readers that did not catch up with the last earnings, I thought this would be helpful: 1Q25 revenue grew 19% y/y on a constant currency [CC] basis and is tracking above management’s FY25 CC revenue guidance of 15–17%. Importantly, like in 4Q24, the growth was across all regions—the Americas grew 16% y/y, EMEA was up 17% y/y, and APAC accelerated sharply to 47% y/y growth. By channel, B2B revenue grew 30% y/y, while DTC grew 10% y/y.

This performance itself is solid proof that BIRK is not seeing any form of demand slowdown. In fact, management’s tone and commentary during the call suggest that underlying demand remains very strong. Specifically, they noted “zero global demand destruction” and no order cancellations across their wholesale network. In fact, order books remain >20% above current supply, and that is across every region. If you consider that multiple headline news have indicated poor retail spending and a decline in discretionary spending, this suggests that BIRK is winning a ton of relative market share.

DTC Strategy Building Momentum into 2H

BIRK's growth outlook remains robust, in my view, with direct-to-consumer [DTC] being the main driver. While DTC growth came in at only 10% y/y for 1Q25, this figure should accelerate in the coming quarters, supported by both new store rollouts. BIRK ended 4Q24 with 71 stores, and management has plans to bring that total to ~100 by the end of FY25, which implies ~40% increase in footprint within a year. We know that underlying demand is strong, so if we assume revenue/store stays flat, this implies DTC revenue could grow by ~40% in FY25, which means the coming quarters growth has to accelerate meaningfully. The momentum at the digital front adds another layer to this DTC accelerating growth story. BIRK now has 8.8 million DTC members, up ~30% y/y, and these members spend ~30% more per transaction vs. non-members. We know that this membership strategy is clearly becoming a growth driver, as ~50% of DTC revenue comes from members.

What this tells me is that BIRK is enjoying a very powerful flywheel. Here is how I see it: more members mean higher transaction value, increased purchase frequency, and lower customer acquisition costs [CAC]. And what makes this even more compelling is that BIRK already has a very efficient customer acquisition strategy. This membership strategy would lower it by even more. Specifically, management noted that 90% of new customers are acquired through unpaid channels, with 60% of those coming via word-of-mouth. The combination of these means that BIRK is not only able to see better demand (higher revenue growth), it also improves customer lifetime value [CLTV] (i.e., better margins). And the beauty is that this entire cycle is a self-reinforcing loop that keeps getting better as BIRK scales.

Taken together, I think this sets up a really compelling case for DTC growth reacceleration.

The Pasewalk factory has reached an inflection point

The other notable margin driver here is BIRK's manufacturing footprint, particularly the ramp-up at its Pasewalk factory. In the call, management confirmed that utilization is now at an inflection point, and starting in 2Q25, they expect the facility to begin contributing positively to gross margins. The expectation is that by FY26, BIRK should recapture the full 150 bps of margin pressure seen in FY24 as the factory reaches a normalized utilization rate. While 150 bps may seem a little, remember that this is a business with relatively high fixed costs. A 150 bps expansion at the gross margin level equates to a sizable impact on the bottom line. Here is an illustration. If 1Q25 had that 150 bps of gross margin tailwind, gross profit would have increased by ~EUR5.4 million. Assuming fixed costs stay the same, this would translate into a ~7% increase in EBIT and a 10% increase in adjusted earnings. And if we compound that impact with the revenue growth strength over the next few quarters, the impact on earnings is likely to be much more.

Valuation

BIRK’s forward PE valuation is a lot more palatable than before. The stock is trading near its all-time low of 22x forward PE, a big discount from its average of 29x. Consensus estimates are also not incredibly tough to meet (~26% CAGR from FY24 to FY28) given growth momentum (FYI: 1Q25 EPS grew almost 100%) and margin expansion potential. Now, if we assume BIRK can deliver EUR3.1 adj EPS in FY28, and the stock doesn’t re-rate, the share price is worth ~EUR68, which represents a 42% upside. However, I think the more probable case is BIRK seeing at least a mean reversion as the market prices in the strong earnings growth potential. If multiple goes back to 29x, the upside is even more attractive.

Tariff Risks

The biggest risk is how BIRK would be impacted by the tariffs, particularly in the US. The indirect impact is that consumer demand may be significantly weakened as inflation spikes up, which impacts demand for BIRK goods. I cannot say for sure demand will not be impacted, but with Trump showing signs of reversing his sweeping tariff plans, this bear case may not materialize.

Conclusion

I upgrade my recommendation to a buy rating for BIRK. The recent derating has made the stock meaningfully more attractive. The business fundamentals remain solid, with DTC growth set to accelerate and margins to expand as Pasewalk ramps up. At ~22x forward P/E, I think the upside is attractive even if multiples do not re-rate higher.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/13/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: First Interstate BancSystem: Reasons To Remain Bearish Are Still There
data.description:
Back in May of last year, one company that I decided to downgrade was First Interstate BancSystem (NASDAQ:FIBK). I was turned off by its low asset quality and a decline in deposits that the bank was exhibiting. While shares were cheap compared to other similar firms and were attractive on a price to book basis, they weren't attractive relative to earnings. An overall decline in revenue and profits has recently weighed on the firm as well. Since downgrading the company from a ‘hold’ to a ‘sell’, the stock has fallen by 5.1%. That's almost double the 2.6% drop the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. As much as I would love to upgrade the company, given the most recent financial data available, that is just not in the cards. Those who want a firm that's cheap relative to its net assets, this might be a valid play. But for investors that want a quality enterprise, I would look elsewhere.

The picture remains disappointing

The most recent data that we have regarding First Interstate BancSystem covers the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. To start with, we should probably touch on its balance sheet. During this time, the enterprise had deposits of $23.02 billion. This actually represents a decline compared to the $23.32 billion in deposits the business ended 2023 with. However, it is up from the $22.81 billion it had on its books in the first quarter of 2024. That was the data that the business had out at the time that I last wrote about it. So in that respect, at least there has been some improvement. Year over year, the company's deposits dropped because of weakness across major categories like non-interest-bearing deposits, demand deposits, and time and other deposits. The good news is that the firm had no brokered deposits at the end of either year. The only increases in enjoyed came from savings deposits, which grew from $7.78 billion to $7.83 billion, and time deposits that had $250,000 or more in them. These time deposits expanded from $811.6 million to $825 million.

Loans are also an important part of the balance sheet that deserve attention. At the end of 2023, First Interstate BancSystem had $18.05 billion worth. This number fell to $17.64 billion by the end of last year. The largest chunk of its loan portfolio involves commercial real estate. About 52% of its loans fall under that category. This is followed up by commercial loans at 16% and residential real estate at 12%. The other categories of loans that the company has include construction real estate, agriculture, consumer, and agriculture real estate. I know that investors have been worried about exposure to office properties given the high vacancy rates that they have had over the last few years. The good news is that, if we exclude medical properties that have office space to them, only about 5.9% of the company's loans involve office assets.

Naturally, there are other aspects of the balance sheet we should touch on as well. For instance, at the end of 2024, First Interstate BancSystem had $7.74 billion invested in securities. This was down from the $9.05 billion reported the previous year. Over the same window of time, cash and cash equivalents did grow from $578 million to $896.6 million. Meanwhile, debt totaled $2.22 billion. What we can see by looking at the firm's data is that the decline in loans and securities was done at least in part in order to pay down this debt. That's because, back in 2023, First Interstate BancSystem had debt of $3.51 billion.

When it comes to the income statement, the picture has been disappointing in my opinion. For the final quarter of 2024, First Interstate BancSystem generated net interest income of $182.2 million. That's down from the $204.1 million reported the previous year. This was in spite of the fact that the company saw its net interest margin rise from 3.01% to 3.20%. Part of the problem was that the firm's provision for credit losses spiked from $5.4 million to $33.7 million. Furthermore, the value of its assets dropped from $30.67 billion to $29.14 billion. A larger decline in the value of liabilities still enabled its book value of equity to climb from $3.23 billion to $3.30 billion. But that was not enough to offset the other weaknesses I mentioned. For the year as a whole, net interest income contracted from $853.6 million to $760.4 million. For this time, the company did suffer a drop in its net interest margin from 3.14% to 3.04%. Meanwhile, the provision for credit losses jumped from $32.2 million to $67.8 million.

We have seen some strength when it comes to non-interest income. From 2023 to 2024, it expanded from $147 million to $178.1 million, with the final quarter of the year seeing an expansion from $44.5 million to $47 million. But this was largely made possible by the fact that, in 2023, the company booked a $23.5 million loss on investment securities. Had it not been for this, the rise in non-interest income would have been only $7.6 million. For the final quarter of 2024, net profits fell to $52.1 million from $61.5 million, with the profit for the year as a whole dropping from $257.5 million to $226 million.

I don't have a problem with a decline in revenue and profits if shares are cheap enough. But to me, they aren't. In the chart above, you can see that First Interstate BancSystem is trading at a price to earnings multiple of 11.9. This is above the 6 to 10 range that I typically like to see. However, this is not a ridiculous trading multiple compared to other similar businesses. In that same chart, you can see the firm stacked up against five such enterprises. Two of the five ended up being cheaper than it is on this basis. In the chart below, meanwhile, I decided to compare First Interstate BancSystem to the same five companies using both the price to book multiple and the price to tangible book multiple. On a price to book basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it comes down to the price to tangible book multiple, only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is.

These price to book multiples would generally be attractive to me. However, one thing that I have a problem with is that the asset quality of the business is lower than I would like it to be. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets compared to the same five businesses. The 0.70% reading that we get here is below the 1% minimum that I typically prefer. Furthermore, four of the five competitors are higher than it is on this basis. And in the subsequent chart, you can see that when it comes to return on equity, the 6.22% that the business has, which is significantly below the 10% minimum I like to see, places it at the bottom of the group.

Takeaway

At this time, I see no reason to become optimistic about First Interstate BancSystem. If shares were cheaper, I might be more neutral on the business. The same would hold true if asset quality was high. But given where the company is, in addition to the broader economic uncertainty that we are now facing, I believe that maintaining a ‘sell’ rating is appropriate right now.

data.title: Toast: In-House R&D Makes The Business More Agile
data.description:
Toast (NYSE:TOST) looks overvalued from an absolute perspective and relative perspective. But leading companies with strong generation of FCF/EC, shareholder friendly policies, independent from other companies (Doesn’t acquire or depend on third parties for product innovation) and with considerable growth opportunities rarely are at fair value. I rate Toast stock a buy.

Background

Toast has been able to conquer the US market without any major acquisitions. A very hard feat for a company that generates most of its revenue via payments processing.

In fact, payment processing is a tough market because multiple banks and fintechs offer commoditized take rates. A large share of the payments market is related to businesses that wish to keep their operations simple while enjoying low costs.

Toast, with higher take rates, is able to consolidate part of the US market using software and equipment that provides advanced analytics and process automation. Moreover, Toast hasn’t expanded with major acquisitions like we typically see from its competitor Shift4 (FOUR).

Toast products were mainly developed in house. This company has a powerful R&D ability that is translating into strong products and a powerful brand.

Strategy

Sell to profitable restaurants a platform of vertically integrated solutions that produce automation and added value analytics. Toast focuses on profitable restaurants that don’t mind significant fixed costs and above market take rates. The company boasts under its portfolio over half of the US Michelin star-rated restaurants. This shows the power of Toast’s solution to deliver premium customer experiences with streamlined processes and added features. At the same time, Toast’s restaurants are faster at processing clients, increasing table turnover and thus revenue. The costs of the restaurants are also minimized through back-office automation.

In addition to automation, Toast’s clients have access to the largest dataset of SMB and mid-market restaurants, providing great benchmarking and valuable analytics in this volatile industry. The data advantage helps Toast’s restaurants to make better decisions in procurement, operations, employees’ salaries and restaurant menus, translating into better strategical and tactical decisions with more lean operations. Consequently, producing more revenue and lower costs.

Toast applies its solution with a large focus and detail, touching on all restaurant operations. Takeout, kitchen operations, ordering processes, and payment processes are streamlined, providing a very vertical solution for its restaurant merchants.

In the latest earnings call, the company’s management said this:

From day one, our vertical strategy has served us well by focusing on solving the needs of restaurants better than anybody else in the world. This has allowed us to deepen our market share and establish a leadership position across SMB and mid-market restaurants here in the US.

Provide competitive rates relative to its product quality to take over the US market and thus build a data moat. Toast’s take rates should be more expensive than banks or other legacy fintechs. However, if we compare Toast’s package to other comparable packages like Shift4’s SkyTab, we can understand that Toast actually provides a very competitive solution. That’s also a reason why the company is increasing so fast and organically.

According to this link, Toast provides a take rate of 2.49% on the transacted amount, while Shift4’s SkyTab provides a take rate of 2.75%. Toast fixed costs should be higher than SkyTab. But for mid-market and SMB’s customers, that generate a lot of volume, TOST’s package is much more attractive. Toast should also be better for restaurants that need to make more strategic and tactical decisions due to better analytics, compared to SkyTab.

Financials

Toast produces much lower gross margins than Shift4. One of the reasons is explained previously. Toast’s take rates are inferior in the restaurant business, compared to Shift4’s. Shift4 is also more diversified into higher gross margins businesses like hotels and stadiums. Toast also provides its physical products below cost to win over the clients, affecting gross margins. Both companies’ gross margins have increased significantly in the past year.

Toast has a much more asset light business, with much less money invested in Goodwill and intangible assets. Despite generating much lower FCFexSBC, while spending almost 6 times Shift4’s SBC, its balance sheet is so light that it produces more FCFexSBC/EC than Shift4.

Shift4 is expected to grow faster in the next 3 years. The growth is fueled by acquisitions that will be constrained by debt levels. Toast should have lower growth, but is much higher quality because it is organic. If Toast is able to pivot to international markets with good execution, its growth estimates should go higher.

In the net debt department, Toast has 8% of the market cap in cash while Shift4 has 25% of the market cap in net debt

On the Price to FCFexSBC, Shift4 is massively undervalued in comparison to Toast. However, Toast is showing friendly shareholder policies. Indeed, the SBC is reducing as the company grows revenue. Thus, TOST’s high multiple should compress a lot in the future.

Opportunities

As mentioned before, Toast is generating a large data moat. Its focus on expanding the US market via lower relative take rates. Then, absorbing data from the restaurants to support strategic and tactical decision-making through decision support systems, was a very well executed strategy that is providing a lot of differentiation and brand recognition.

The automation and process improvement features are nice to have. But, for me, the segment that is taking market share in the premium restaurants is the advanced analytics product. That other companies like Shift4, at least from what I searched, is lacking.

In the latest earnings call, the company management said this:

This benchmarking tool has been a game-changer for many of our customers, including our team at Äta, a full-service restaurant in Louisville, who have used our insights to enhance their menu, add new specials, and even change their hours of operation to grow their business. For example, they noticed that chicken sales were underrepresented in restaurants near them by using this data, and used that to add and market a fried chicken special on a slower night that boosted Tuesday night revenues by 40%; that's the power of our data.

Toast, with this differentiated advantage, should take more market share in the US and abroad

Risks

Tariffs will overall affect restaurant consumption, including consumption in Toast’s merchants. Wealthy individuals are already losing massive amounts of money in the markets, which will affect how much they spend in restaurants.

The other potential risk is Shift4, which has more than 100 thousand restaurants in the US, developing an advanced analytics and benchmarking product that will reduce Toast’s differentiation.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. To be conservative, I assumed an initial FCF margin of 6%. Then it expands 0.5% every year. To generate the terminal multiple, I’m using a terminal growth rate of 4.7%. The terminal growth rate is obtained by summing a real GDP growth rate of 2.5% and an inflation rate (since 2000) of 2.2%, resulting in the number 4.7%. I also take into consideration the share count to calculate the FCF per share. It expands by 3% every year. With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current share price.

The model outputs an initial growth rate of 21.9% that reduces 5% every year, reaching 13.8% in 2034. Considering the sell-side analysts’ estimations for the next three years, Toast is somewhat overvalued. Despite this, Toast is a high-quality company, with potential for long-term growth through internationalization, entering new markets and increasing take rates. High-quality companies usually never trade on fair value. Thus, right now, Toast stock price looks attractive.

Conclusion

Toast has a much more R&D, built in-house, heavy business strategy that is showing results. I also like the fact that Toast’s take rates are lower than Shift4’s because it allows the former to take market share and build a data moat. Moreover, the company brand becomes more diffused by taking market share while building good products. Shift4 is also attractive because it is very undervalued and has been executing well its strategy of acquiring and expanding. Overall, I think Toast will outperform Shift4 in the future because of in-house R&D, which is much more agile at dealing with market changes and customer feedback. I like both companies from a buying point of view, but I prefer longing Toast over Shift4. I rate Toast a buy.

data.title: Expand Energy: Fairly Priced For Data Center And LNG Growth
data.description:
Thesis

Expand Energy (NASDAQ:EXE) is embarking on its first full year as the combined entity following the merger of Chesapeake Energy and Southwestern Energy. Despite multiple drivers from natural gas consumption via data center growth and LNG exports, the share price for EXE appropriately prices in any future improvement in natural gas pricing.

Fundamentally, the business models of data center operation and LNG exports are based on the assumption of cheap natural gas. In both cases, a blow out in natural gas prices creates demand destruction and would inevitably create a cyclical market downturn.

As a result, modeling natural gas prices above mid-cycle prices exposes investors to unnecessary long-term risk. Using a forward curve of $3.75-$4.00/MCF, EXE generates an FCF yield between 10-12%. This is reasonable but hardly a bargain. As a result, I rate the company as a HOLD.

Natural Gas Markets Are Different Than Crude Oil

Natural gas is different from crude oil in a variety of ways. To start with, consumption of the fuel source is significantly impacted by the weather. Natural gas is relied upon for both heating and cooling, as a significant portion of the power grid has converted from coal to natural gas fueled electric power generation.

Secondly, the cost of production varies significantly across the three major producing basins. The Permian, Marcellus, and Haynesville shales all have unique attributes that drive production and influence pricing.

Starting in the Permian, production of natural gas is a byproduct of oil production and is the second-largest natural gas producing basin. Despite this, well economics and drilling activity are purely driven by the price of oil. This is partially caused by maxed out pipeline capacity that causes producers to accept low or even negative prices for their product to ensure oil production is not compromised.

The dry gas basins of the Marcellus and Haynesville operate in a more traditional manner. However, even here, the difference in production cost between the two basins causes the Marcellus to operate more as a baseload provider and the Haynesville as a swing provider when prices elevate.

This effectively places a semi-flexible ceiling on natural gas prices, as both basins have shut in wells over the last year in response to low gas prices. Additionally, a very high DUC count in both basins allows new supply to be brought online with lower near-term investment.

Price Blowouts Are Bad For Business

Natural gas markets have two highly anticipated avenues for growth in the form of both LNG exports and increased electricity demand from AI data centers. AI mania has likely painted a dollar sign in many investors' heads. Unfortunately, these drivers are formulated around the concept of cheap energy.

Let's start with LNG exports. This business exists because there is such a substantial price differential between US sourced natural gas and the rest of the world that it is economical to liquify and ship the cooled and compressed liquid thousands of miles. The size of that spread is what incentivizes molecules to cross oceans.

As more LNG terminals come online (both in the US and Qatar), more supply becomes available to global markets, decreasing the price to import. At the same time, domestic prices increased from increased demand. The resulting spread compression creates an inefficient capital cycle where producers are incentivized to increase production at a time when liquefaction spread begins to shrink.

US natural gas prices significantly above the $5-$6/mcf range would ultimately be destructive for LNG exports. Above these values, the combined cost of supply, liquefaction, and transportation becomes less competitive. As spreads decline and production ramps in response to rising domestic prices, a cyclical downturn would likely result from an oversupplied market.

Data Centers Love Cheap Electricity

Cheap sources of power are also central to the build out in AI data centers. As I have detailed in previous analysis of power provider Talen Energy (TLN), grid operators are forecasting significant growth in energy demand. This growth has appeared rapidly, as indicated in the difference between the blue and green projections by PJM (the nation's largest grid operator).

This demand is beginning to attract new investment into the region. The most recent example of which is one of the largest power plant announcements to date. Developers recently announced plans for a 4.5 GW combined cycle natural gas power plant to be built on a data center campus in Homer City, PA. Fueling this growth requires significant base load power that only natural gas can provide.

Operating costs for data centers would also be challenged in a similar scenario as described for LNG exports. Increasing natural gas prices to $5/mcf represents a 43% increase from today's strip price. The associated increase in electric rates could cause some data center projects to no long be economic, again resulting in demand destruction.

All of this is to demonstrate that investors should not be banking on a blow out in natural gas prices as a pathway to market beating returns. Prices above $5/MCF marks the approach to a cyclical top. Above this price level, demand destruction begins to set in, while producers have already started to ramp up production.

Similar to what was experienced in the oil market of 2022-2024, the increase in activity eventually brings prices back down. This emphasizes the point of analyzing a producer such as EXE on a mid-cycle price deck versus a top market price.

Free Cash Flow Analysis

For this analysis, I have selected a mid-cycle price point in the $3.75-$4/mcf range. This level is suitable to justify production in the Marcellus as well as top-tier inventory in the Haynesville. Using EXE's 2025 operating expense guidance, we can develop a free cash flow model of the combined company.

In the example below, we can see that the current share price and a strip price of $3.75/mcf produces a free cash flow yield of about 10%. At the high end of my mid-cycle price deck ($4/mcf), this improves to about 12%. This is reasonable for mid-cycle conditions but also indicates that the stock is fairly valued.

Risks

The energy markets have seen significant turbulence over the last several weeks. Escalations in tariffs have caused crude prices to decline over 15% to around $60/barrel. As a result, margins in oil dominate basins have declined significantly.

If this is sustained for any significant amount of time, I would expect operators to begin dialing down activity in key basins such as the Permian. As I mentioned earlier, gas production out of this region follows oil volumes. As activity declines, natural gas production will follow suit, regardless of the price of natural gas.

This could significantly bolster the prospects of EXE's dry gas assets. Such an event would require the Marcellus and Haynesville shales to carry a larger portion of total demand.

An acute version of this can be seen when winter storms impact the gulf coast region. Winter storms in 2021, 2022, and 2024 have shown the ability to knock off significant natural gas volumes.

The resulting price spikes can be seen below. While much less severe than the 2021 event, prices in February 2022 flirted with $8/mcf.

While I don't expect anything as drastic as shown above, declining activity in the Permian would give natural gas the ability to sustain a long-term bull run. In this context, EXE would be able to generate higher levels of free cash flow than what I have projected in the FCF model.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. The differences in cost structures between the three major natural gas producing basins in the US give the commodity multiple levers to increase supply.

2. A high DUC count in the Marcellus and Haynesville also gives producers cheaper and faster options to increase supply in response to rising natural gas prices.

3. Key demand growth drivers in LNG exports and AI data center expansions are built under the premise of cheap energy supplies. Demand destruction could result from prices exceeding $5/MCF.

4. With these items in mind, investors should value natural gas producers at a mid-cycle price. Investing for the market top presents risk to capital.

5. At a mid-cycle price of $3.75-$4/MCF, EXE generates a free cash flow yield of 10-12%. This indicates that EXE is fairly valued.

6. I rate EXE a HOLD.

data.title: ZCN:CA: The Bull Case For Canada And The Issue
data.description:
Canada's stagnant economic growth in the past decade has led many to dub it as a "lost decade" where GDP per capita in U.S. dollars only grew 1.39% from 2014 to 2023. At the same time, the U.S. GDP per capita saw an increase of 52.96%, highlighting how far Canada had fallen. And while Canada's stock market has fared better than its GDP per capita growth, investing in the S&P 500 would have generated more than double the returns in the S&P/TSX Composite Index in the last ten years. Fortunately for Canadians, there are signs of economic change coming. Trump's trade war has been a wake-up call and shifted focus in Canada toward building a stronger economy. Almost all discussion in major political parties have shifted toward how Canada can become more self-sustaining and the need to reduce the country's reliance on the U.S. This also comes at a time where energy is becoming increasingly more important with the rapid development and adoption of technological advancements such as AI, robotics, and electric vehicles ("EV").

That said, all of this is speculation until policies and real changes have been enacted. Before then, buying an ETF that tracks the Canadian stock market via the S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index, such as the BMO S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index ETF (ZCN:CA), comes with a few issues. First, it's undeniable that the U.S. is and will likely continue to be Canada's largest trading partner. The proximity and shared attributes between the two countries naturally make them a good fit as allies. Hence, Trump's tariffs and falling oil prices will cause more pain for ZCN:CA until real changes are implemented. There's also a consideration of whether you want to be buying ZCN:CA in the first place. For overall Canadian market exposure, ZCN:CA is one of your best buy-and-holds, but there is a strong case for being more specific and opting for individual stocks and sectors. Much of the S&P/TSX index is made up of cyclical and commodity-linked companies that are often value plays, and the index is not as reliably diversified as other indices such as the S&P 500. When diversification introduces more single-stock risk than it does protection, it would be more worthwhile to spend more time researching and opt for specific sectors and individual stocks instead of the index as a whole. So, although I am bullish on Canada's economy over the long term, it is pinpointed to specific sectors and stocks. ZCN:CA is one of the best funds for simple, low-cost exposure to Canadian stocks, but the S&P/TSX Composite Index's current selection of stocks makes it better for investors to look for specific sector or individual stock investment opportunities. Thus, I rate ZCN:CA a Hold.

Shifts In Canadian Politics

A good sign for Canada's economy is that the two major political parties most likely to win the election - Mark Carney's Liberals and Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives - have both centered their policies around the economy, thus increasing the chance for more favorable business operating conditions. In a way, the Trump administration has managed to create a common enemy for Canadians to focus on and unite much of the country. For example, on March 21, 2025, Prime Minister Mark Carney committed to table legislation by July 1, 2025, to eliminate all federal barriers to interprovincial trade and labour mobility and to remove all federal exemptions under the Canada Free Trade Agreement. In this meeting, Carney also outlined numerous developments to support economic development in Canada. The first is to create a First Mile Fund with the goal of building transportation networks to help connect energy extraction sites to rail lines and roads. The second is a "one window" approval process aimed at expediting processes for new large-scale, national-interest infrastructure developments. Third, the government is contributing up to $200M to support the Cedar LNG project in Kitimat, British Columbia, that will help Canada export LNG to Asia markets. Lastly, an investment of $175M was announced to support Hudson Bay Railway and Port of Churchill developments to eventually open up more assets for global trade. Other important developments are a changing sentiment toward Ontario's Ring of Fire where political leaders now see an opportunity to invest and develop the mines for precious minerals such as chromite, cobalt, nickel, copper, and platinum. Conservative leader, Pierre Poilievre, for example, has promised to green-light all Ring of Fire federal permits within six months of taking office, which would be unbelievable considering that the Ring of Fire has not had any meaningful development happen despite being discovered in 2007.

And truly, more favorable investing and development business environments may be all that Canada needs. With how AI and automation is continuing to shape the world, Canada's abundance of energy and natural resources could become vital. AI development and other things like a shift to EVs have huge energy and resource needs, not to mention that replacing human labor with AI and robots is essentially physically replacing humans with electricity and the resources used to build the robots. For example, the IEA in its "Mineral requirements for clean energy transitions" report discussed some of the critical minerals required for clean energy transitions and showed the importance of each resource in various energy technologies. Many of these resources are widely available in Canada, and it's really been a matter of opening up development to these projects.

ZCN:CA - Great Way To Get Canadian Exposure

To invest in the Canadian stock market as a whole, one of the best ways is through ZCN:CA. This is an ETF that tracks the S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index. This index does not perfectly track the Canadian stock market as a whole, but it covers approximately 95% of the Canadian equities market and gives the best overall representation. Currently, ZCN:CA has 219 holdings, and most of them are concentrated in financials, energy, industrials, and basic materials. The fund offers a 0.05% management fee, which is among the lowest you can get from a Canadian ETF alongside iShares Core S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index ETF (XIC:CA) and Global X S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index Corporate Class ETF (HXCN:CA). Both competitors offer minimal differences other than the underlying issuer of iShares and Global X versus BMO, and do not provide significant advantages unless your particular brokerage offers lower trading fees on any of the funds. Although I will make a small note that despite all three funds having a management fee of 0.05%, the actual management expense ratio ("MER") is 0.06% for ZCN:CA and XIC:CA but remains 0.05% for HXCN:CA. MER in Canada typically adds a sales tax charge on top of the management fee. HXCN:CA once had a MER of 0.06% like the others, which suggests a rounding problem. The 0.01% is unlikely to make a difference in the grand scheme of things.

Outside of XIC:CA and HXCN:CA, other popular ways to passively track the Canadian stock market is through S&P/TSX 60 linked funds or through a FTSE Canada All Cap Index fund. Both, however, are less diversified. The S&P/TSX 60 is the top 60 stocks by market cap within the S&P/TSX Composite, while the FTSE Canada All Cap Index is derived from the FTSE Global Equity Index Series and excludes investable assets like limited partnerships. The S&P/TSX Composite is also a little broader with its requirements, which is why ZCN:CA has 219 holdings, while the Vanguard FTSE Canada All Cap Index ETF (VCN:CA) has 160 holdings. While more diversification does not necessarily equate to better long-term performance, you do reduce single-ticker risk, which is valuable for investors who aren't closely tracking each company and sector.

The Issues With ZCN:CA

Now, although ZCN:CA is one of the best ways to gain exposure to the overall Canadian stock market at low costs, it has to be a long-term investment. Everything we had previously discussed regarding Canada's future economy are long-term ideas. Approval for large-scale developments and new trade relationships are things that happen over the span of weeks, months, or even years. Building the actual projects would likely take even longer, and the actual ROI is another story to look at. In the meantime, the reality Canada faces is Trump's tariffs and falling oil prices, which will hurt an already stagnant economy.

As you can see from the chart above, however, it's not the entire S&P/TSX Composite that's performing poorly. The material sector in the S&P/TSX Composite has been propping the index up with a 15.11% positive total return YTD due to gold's rise and is being offset by losses primarily driven by the information technology and energy sectors.

The issue with ZCN:CA and the S&P/TSX Composite is that there are much more limited investment opportunities compared to a U.S. counterpart like the S&P 500. Information technology stocks currently occupy 9.17% of ZCN:CA's portfolio with ten stocks. 4.37% of that allocation, however, comes from SHOP:CA.

Similarly, QSR:CA is the only restaurant stock in this index. This isn't a testament to QSR:CA or SHOP:CA's value, but rather how limited the S&P/TSX Composite Index can be in certain sectors. And because of these limitations, it can make more sense to focus on specific sectors where Canada does have an abundance of companies and be more efficient with your investment theses. For this article, as an example, Canadian political changes to greenlight and build various projects have most of the benefit directed at energy and material stocks. Financial companies, utilities, and industrial companies would naturally see benefits from these changes as well, but I can't see how it would change much for Canadian information technology stocks and SHOP:CA in particular.

Long-Term Opportunities In Canada

Although the Canadian economy has been stagnant for the last decade and things only seem to be getting worse due to Trump's tariffs and falling oil prices, the benefit is that it has shifted Canadians to think more economically. Sentiments surrounding large infrastructure projects have become more positive and are crucial not just for a more self-sustaining economy independent of the U.S., but potentially for the world as AI advancements and technological shifts increase our consumption of energy and raw materials. That said, many of these projects are only planned, and it could take years before we start seeing actual ROI. This means an investment in Canada only makes sense if you're investing for the long term. And for those that are, ZCN:CA is a great choice due to its low-cost, passively managed approach, though I would opt for a more active approach if time and energy allows. I rate ZCN:CA a Hold.

data.title: DPST: Euphoria Turns To Despair In A Mere 6 Months
data.description:
Introduction

November 2024. The Direxion Daily Regional Banks Bull 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:DPST) has just surged by ~40% in a single month following the U.S. presidential election, with investors expecting tax cuts and deregulation to boost regional bank performance.

April 2025. Investors are worried about the U.S. economic outlook and the impact tariffs will have on inflation. DPST has slumped by ~53% so far in 2025, while the unleveraged SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE) is down ~18%:

What will the narrative look like in October 2025? I would argue somewhere in the middle. I previously covered DPST back in January 2025 with a Buy rating, arguing that regional banks remained attractively valued relative to other U.S. financials. This remains the case today, with regional banks trading at just 9.44x their forward earnings estimates.

In this article, I highlight risks to reaching 2025 earnings targets and why 2026 could bring a turnaround for regional banks. Taking into account undemanding valuations and pessimistic investor sentiment, I confirm my Buy rating for DPST.

ETF Overview

You can access all DPST details on the Direxion website here. The Direxion Daily Regional Banks Bull 3X Shares ETF seeks to deliver 300% (3x) the return of the S&P Regional Banks Select Industry Index. The same index is tracked by the unleveraged SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF.

As both the Direxion and index websites visualize index holdings as of the end of Q1 2025 (i.e. before the bulk of the recent volatility) I think a neat way to look at DPST exposures is to actually to examine the website of State Street which are reponsible for the unleveraged KRE ETF and update information with a smaller delay.

The underlying index has 28.45% of its assets allocated to just 10 regional banks, as shown in the snippet below:

DPST's expense ratio stands at 0.90%, in line with other 300% leveraged ETFs, but well above the 0.35% expense ratio for the unleveraged KRE ETF.

Index valuation

As you may expect, valuations have come down quite significantly since my previous article. The index tracked by DPST and KRE trades at a trailing P/E ratio of ~10.98x, while the forward P/E stands at ~9.44x:

Looking at data from my two previous DPST articles, we see that this is the cheapest regional banks have been over the past six months:

Period Forward P/E
October 2024 11.83
January 2025 13.10
April 2025 9.44

Source: Author calculations

Putting the data from the above table into context, the iShares U.S. Financials ETF (IYF) trades at a trailing P/E of ~14.86x, indicating regional banks trade at a discount of 25-30% relative to U.S. financials, providing a wide margin of safety for investors.

Risks to reaching earnings targets

As explained in the previous paragraph, regional banks are very cheap should they meet their forward earnings projections. However, given the uncertain economic backdrop for the U.S. economy, it is fair to say these forward earnings will be hard to reach. Amid rising recession fears, banks have to frontload reserve build so that when debtors default later in 2025 or 2026, they have adequate capital/provisions to cover these losses.

Large banks like JPMorgan Chase (JPM) reported just that in their Q1 2025 results. However, regional banks typically have less exposure to capital markets businesses, which cushioned the reserve build at JPM. As such, the near-term outlook for regional banks' earnings in Q1-Q2 2025 is quite muted, as I expect them to frontload reserve build as is required under banking regulations.

I would also note that markets have recently priced in more rate cuts in 2025-2026, which will likely impact the net interest income of regional banks. For instance, futures prices already indicate the Fed will be close to its 3% neutral rate in December 2026 (which is just 20 months away):

2026 - a turnaround for regional banks

As explained above, reaching earnings estimates in 2025 will be quite challenging for regional banks. This should change in 2026 as a number of factors are likely to boost performance:

  • Reserve build that dominates 2025 results is in the rear-view mirror
  • Banks adjust policy to faster Fed rate cuts, stabilizing net interest income
  • U.S. economic outlook improves as there is (hopefully) more clarity on tariffs
  • Lending growth picks up as interest rates decline
  • Republicans seek to stabilize the economy as midterms could challenge their House and Senate majorities, focusing on tax cuts and deregulation

All in all, while there is material risk to regional banks reaching their 2025 earnings estimates, I believe you can reasonably assume they will do so in 2026. Hence, buying regional banks in April 2025 at a circa 9.5x 2026 earnings multiple is not a bad deal, in my opinion. Thus, I confirm my Buy rating for DPST, notwithstanding risks related to near-term earnings.

ETF Risks

Aside from the principal risk of defaults and credit losses should the U.S. economy enter recession (which is arguably priced in to an extent), you should note the significant near-term losses that 300% leveraged ETFs such as DPST can deliver. As explained in my first DPST article (which, as it turns out, was appropriately titled "Regional Banks Offer Strong Returns Until Crisis Strikes"), DPST never recovered from its 90% COVID-19 slump in just 40 days during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The recent decline from the November 2024 peak amounts to ~69%, and there is no certainty that investors will look through the Q1-Q2 2025 result build. As such, I continue to advocate an active risk management approach when investing in 300% leveraged ETFs.

Investors in DPST should also consider the effect of beta slippage (the drop in price leveraged ETFs experience in sideways markets). Combined with the elevated expense ratio, this results in significant net asset value erosion.

You can read more about the risk-return profile and risks associated with leveraged ETFs on the SEC website here.

Conclusion

Markets are moved by both emotions and fundamentals. I believe regional banks currently trade at an attractive forward P/E multiple of 9.44x (although full earnings potential will likely only be unlocked in 2026). At the same time investor expectations are quite downbeat, with the CNN Fear & Greed index sitting at 13 out of 100, indicating Extreme Fear (although it is up from even lower readings this month):

Warren Buffet famously said "Be fearful when others are greedy, be greedy when others are fearful". As such, taking into account attractive valuations and very low investor sentiment, I confirm my Buy rating for DPST.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: C3.ai's Cheaper Valuations Are Negated By Mixed Macro/Geopolitical Events
data.description:
C3.ai Is Finally Attractively Valued After The Deep Selloff, Albeit Likely Impacted By The Mixed Macro/Geopolitical Events

We previously covered C3.ai (NYSE:AI) in October 2024, discussing its growing relevance during the generative AI boom, as observed in the raised FY2025 guidance and the raised consensus forward estimates, significantly aided by the growing partnerships with Microsoft (MSFT).

Even then, with the stock increasingly expensive, with the same trend also observed in its AI SaaS peers during the ongoing cloud supercycle, we had believed that there was a minimal margin of safety then, worsened by the company's start-up cash burn trends, growing shareholder dilution, and deteriorating balance sheet.

C3.ai 1Y Stock Price

Since then, C3.ai's upward momentum has indeed faltered as it has fallen by -35.7%, with a similar correction also observed in its AI SaaS peers and the wider market in varying degrees.

Much of the headwinds are naturally attributed to the bursting AI bubble by mid-February 2025, worsened by the new US administration's intensified tariff war and the high inflationary environment.

This development naturally highlights the dangers of momentum trading, as C3.ai's previously inflated valuations and sky-high stock prices turn into a double-edged knife upon the reversal in market sentiments.

This is despite the supposed durability in AI boom across semiconductor infrastructure, data centers, and SaaS layers, a similar development observed in C3.ai's double-beat FQ3 '25 performance.

This is with subscription revenues of $85.67M (+5.5% QoQ/+21.6% YoY) and gross profit margins of 59% (-2.3 points QoQ/+1.3 YoY/-16.2 from FY2020 levels of 75.2%).

Despite the notable gross margin impact from C3.ai's shift from a subscription-based pricing model to a consumption-based pricing model, it is apparent that the decision has triggered a robust top-line growth prospect, significantly aided by the expanded partnerships with MSFT, Amazon (AMZN), and McKinsey & Company QuantumBlack.

The partnerships have directly contributed to the accelerated 46 agreements closed (+74% YoY), compared to the overall sum of 66 agreements in FQ3'25 (+72% YoY), as C3.ai and MSFT are also "engaged in joint sales campaigns to 621 accounts in Europe, Asia, and North and South America."

It is apparent that their ongoing efforts have borne fruit, through the joint hosting of "executive roundtables, virtual fireside chats, and three-day workshops, where we jointly engage hands-on with customers to bring working Enterprise AI applications live in three days," similar to Palantir's (PLTR) AIP boot camp.

These reasons are likely why C3.ai has felt confident to raise their FY2025 guidance, with higher revenues of $388.9M at the midpoint (+25.2% YoY) and stable adj loss from operations at -$92M (inline YoY).

This is compared to the original guidance of $382.5M (+23.1% YoY) and -$110M (-15.9% YoY) offered in the FQ4'23 earnings call, respectively.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus has raised their forward estimates, with C3.ai expected to chart an accelerated top-line growth at a CAGR of +22.5% through FY2027, compared to the original estimates of +19.4%.

These developments underscore why the expanded partnerships and the ongoing AI boom have already triggered renewed top-line growth opportunities for the SaaS company, albeit yet to be bottom-line accretive.

C3.ai Valuations

These reasons are also why C3.ai appears to be rather compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 5.38x, thanks to the moderation from the 1Y mean of 6.89x and 3Y mean of 6.19x.

The same conclusion may also be derived after comparing to its AI SaaS peers, including:

  • SoundHound AI (SOUN) at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 20.03x with a projected 2Y top-line growth at a CAGR of +59.4%,
  • BigBear.ai Holdings, Inc. (BBAI) at 5.83x/3Y CAGR of +20.4%,
  • PLTR at 55.92x/+30.7%,
  • Snowflake (SNOW) at 10.67x/+23.4%,
  • AppLovin (APP) at 16.89x/+19.8%,
  • MSFT at 10.59x/+13.9%, and
  • Google (GOOG) at 4.83x/+10.9%, respectively,

Especially since, C3.ai is expected to generate accelerated top-line growth at a 3Y CAGR of +22.5%.

On the other hand, it is apparent that C3.ai is not expected to generate any meaningful profitability any time soon, despite the raised FY2025 guidance.

Pending a future reversal in its bottom-line performance, it goes without saying that the company is likely to continue its ongoing cash burn and dilutive share-based compensation in the intermediate term.

The same has been observed in the still negative Free Cash Flow generation of -$54.77M on a YTD basis (+49.8% YoY), albeit well-balanced by the stable balance sheet at a net cash position of $724.32M in FQ3 '25 (inline YoY).

So, Is C3.ai Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

C3.ai 4Y Stock Price

For now, the ongoing meltdown has directly triggered C3.ai's correction by -54.9% since the 52-week heights of $45.08, as the stock breaches numerous support levels and trades way below its 50/100/200 day moving averages.

Based on the Enterprise Value of $1.97B by the time of writing and the consensus FY2027 revenue estimates of $571.55M, the stock appears rather compelling at FY2027 EV/Sales valuations of 3.44x as well, nearer to the current sector median of 2.42x.

On the other hand, despite the massive pullback, C3.ai remains highly shorted at 25.8% of its float, worsened by the eye-watering CBOE Volatility Index at 40.72x compared to the 50-days moving averages of 22.14x, suggesting that a floor is unlikely to materialize any time soon.

If anything, readers must note there are numerous risks to its long-term prospects attributed to the ongoing DOGE headwinds on current/future government contracts, since the Federal, Defense and Aerospace/State and Local Government sectors comprise 28.3% of its FQ3'25 bookings (-25.1 points YoY).

For reference, DOGE has already terminated its prior contracts with Accenture (ACN), since they supposedly "do not match the priorities of this president or this department. In other words, they are not a good use of taxpayer dollars."

This development has directly triggered ACN's downgraded consensus adj EPS estimates to a CAGR of +7.8% through FY2027, compared to the original estimates of +14.1%, despite federal services only comprising 8% of its global revenues in FY2024.

At the same time, market analysts are already projecting higher recessionary risks of up to 60% (compared to the prior estimates of 40%), as Reuters also report "large increases in planned layoffs in the technology and retail sectors."

Despite the management's ongoing efforts to diversify its end markets, we believe that the ongoing headwinds may trigger the SaaS sector's elongated sales cycle and impacted top/bottom-line/backlog growth profile, C3.ai included.

As a result of the potential volatility, inherent lack of profitability, sustained share dilution, and uncertain impact from the intensified macro and geopolitical tensions, we prefer to stand on the sidelines and maintain a Hold (Neutral) rating here.

data.title: International General Insurance's Greatest Catalyst Is Its Ability To Manage Its Combined Ratio
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I have previously covered relatively small specialty insurers such as Mercury General (MCY), Skyward Specialty Insurance (SKWD), and Fidelis Insurance Group (FIHL), and International General Insurance Corporation (NASDAQ:IGIC) is no exception. What interests me with IGIC is that as much as it is relatively small compared to the others, its geographical diversification extends to over 200 countries, including Bermuda, EMEA, and Asia Pacific.

What caught my attention about IGIC is that it has an attractive adjusted combined ratio. If you look at its 2024 earnings report, the company recorded a combined ratio of 79.9% compared to 76.7%. In the insurance industry, the most important ratio is the combined ratio, as it evaluates the profitability of the insurance company. According to a report by McKinsey, most insurance companies have their combined ratio greater than 90%. For IGIC, this ratio of 79.9% implies that for every $1.0 of every premium that has been collected by the company, approximately $0.8 is used in paying claims and running the expenses of the company, and the balance is treated as profit before investment income. According to Seeking Alpha's Quant ratings, IGIC has a profitability grade of B+. As we have seen, this is because of its combined ratio. In addition, IGIC is operating at net income margins of 25.07% compared to the sector median of 22.47%.

As a result of the combined ratio, in my opinion, IGIC has been able to create shareholder value. This is shown by its most recent reported ROE of 24.2% and the diluted EPS of $3.19. The graph below also shows that the company has been able to grow its GWP at an average CAGR of 11.2%, while its average combined ratio is 87%. In the chart shown below, I was able to observe a few things:

IGIC seems to have disciplined cycle management. If you look at the long-tail business segment, IGIC has grown the segment from 15% in 2016 to 29% in 2024, especially after it made the decision to shift to more profitable segments in the 8-year comparison period.

Secondly, since the company went public in 2020, IGIC has managed to grow its GWP by an average of 13% y/y and this has resulted in an ROE avg. of 17%.

Combined Ratio as a Catalyst

In my previous articles on Mercury General and Skyward Specialty, I highlighted that while the adjusted combined ratio is an important metric to analyze the profitability of an insurer, looking at the CAT losses might also give you a hint of how well an insurer's risk management framework is. In the diagram below, it is quite clear that IGIC has had a couple of catastrophic losses. And as one would expect them to be large and “catastrophic” to its book, to the point of operating at losses, it hasn't. In fact, since 2021, when IGIC had CAT losses, its combined ratio has been improving from 86% in 2021 to 80% in 2024. In my opinion, this shows that IGIC is conscious about these events and, as a result, strives to have a well-defined and strict risk management framework.

In my opinion, this is a clear sign of a company that operates in a profitable underwriting business, conducts solid risk and stress tests to understand its overall exposure, and is conservative in its reinsurance limits to protect its capital base, profitability, and balance sheet. From a book value perspective, IGIC is a champion in creating shareholder value. Since the IPO, IGIC has had its book value increase from $10.2 to $18.48, resulting in a CAGR of 16%. If you compare the book value and the ROE as of the IPO in 2020, you will see that IGIC has been intentional about creating wealth for its shareholders, and with this trend, I would expect the company to continue increasing its book value as well as its ROE.

Valuation

From a valuation point of view, IGIC is currently trading at a forward P/E of 7.48x compared to its sector median of 11.15x. This represents a 32.51% discount. Additionally, its forward EV/sales ratio shows a 44% reduction from its current ratio of 1.54x against the sector median of 2.76x.

From a peer comparison perspective, while IGIC is trading at a 1.51x P/B ratio, its peer AMERISAFE (AMSF) trades at 3.59x, while Employers Holdings (EIG) trades at 1.07x. ProAssurance (PRA) trades at 0.98x, while United Fire Group (UFCS) and Donegal Group (DGICA) trade at 0.84x and 1.17x, respectively, as shown below.

IGIC Key Risks

As we have seen with previous specialty insurance companies like Mercury General, Skyward, and Fidelis Insurance, one of the biggest risks is an increase in catastrophic losses. This applies to IGIC as well. As we observed in 2017, the combined ratio was badly affected and went to a high of 105%, leading to losses. While these catastrophic events are rare, when they occur, they certainly have a negative effect on the company's balance sheet. As a mitigation measure, IGIC has a solid risk assessment framework and a well-diversified pool of insurance products, both from a product and geographical perspective. Having said that, I would expect them to manage any catastrophic loss event well, given their track record since 2017.

Another big risk I see with IGIC is the cyclicality of the insurance market. With the recently announced tariffs, it is evident that the demand and supply of insurance products will be greatly affected. Here is an excerpt from a report from PwC that highlights that the introduction of tariffs will certainly increase the cost of claims and disrupt the global supply chain.

Alex Bertolotti, Head of Insurance, PwC UK

We believe that the introduction of tariffs will increase the cost of claims, particularly in motor and home, and as a result, there will be pressure to pass these costs on to consumers.

Mohammad Khan, Head of General Insurance, PwC UK

Tariffs on imported construction materials like steel and timber could increase the repair or rebuild costs for structures after being impacted by events like fires, storms, or floods. This will likely add to home and commercial property insurance costs rising.

Final Thoughts

In any insurance business, there is always a risk that your computed premiums may not be able to cover expenses and claims in case of unexpected events. This is specifically when there are unexpected events, such as natural calamities or weather-related events, among others. However, IGIC has set its record straight: whether there is a catastrophic loss event or not, they will pay the claim. And still make money! And, for sure, that's every insurer's dream. The company's ability to manage its risk and reserves, and still price its rates profitably is amazing. As a result, ROE has been increasing, book value per share has been growing organically, and so has the share price. It is for these reasons that I believe IGIC's greatest strength is its ability to maintain its combined ratio below 90%, enabling the company to continue being profitable in the long run.

data.title: Summit Therapeutics: Not Pharmacyclics, Not Yet
data.description:
Summit Therapeutics Topline Summary and Update

Summit Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SMMT) has presented us with one of the biggest hype stories of the biologic's era in cancer medicine, surging out of nowhere to challenge the king behemoths in immunotherapy. "Summit Beats Merck" has been the singular impossible-to-ignore headline over the past year, kicking off an entire conversation about the role of bispecific antibodies, Chinese agents, and the future of pembrolizumab.

And there are a few skeptical voices about the prospects of ivonescimab and the investment thesis for SMMT as it stands in 2025. I am on record as being one of them, and that bearish sentiment, if you want to call it that, has not proven right by any stretch. Today, we got some news that CEO Bob Duggan and his co-CEO Maky Zanganeh exercised their warrants early to acquire nearly 4 million more shares each of the company's stock, which was seen as a strong bullish signal by the markets.

Today, I want to offer some updated thoughts on the company at this stage, highlighting why I think the risk at these levels outweighs the potential reward, despite the many catalysts and the rampant speculation that have driven the meteoric rise of this company.

Big Questions for SMMT – Is HARMONi-2 Really Relevant?

The thesis for SMMT is driven entirely by its stake in the bispecific antibody ivonescimab, which simultaneously binds and blocks PD-1 (the same target as blockbuster antibodies like pembrolizumab and nivolumab) and VEGF (the target of Roche's venerable antibody bevacizumab).

The main thrust of this company's current success lies mainly in the findings from a single study, HARMONi-2, which has been covered extensively by different analysts on Seeking Alpha, including me back when they were first reported. In short, this was the first study to show that a novel drug given alone could beat pembrolizumab in the first-line setting of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), with a near doubling of the progression-free survival.

More recently, we saw the formal publication of these results in The Lancet, offering a glimpse at more data. My hope is that a careful read of these findings will help to answer the following questions:

  1. Did patients live longer and better with ivonescimab?
  2. Are the results translatable to patients outside of China?

For question 1, we have some good and some bad news. First and foremost, overall survival data are not mature, and the authors do not present even an immature readout of those findings, so it remains to be seen whether ivonescimab improves arguably the most important outcome that will determine uptake of the drug in a crowded lung cancer space.

That said, I would be very surprised to see overall survival fail when we have this magnitude of benefit in terms of PFS.

Something else to consider, however, is that the standard of care for NSCLC is not pembrolizumab in patients who do not have high PD-L1 (50% or higher by TPS), and that was over half of the patients in HARMONi-2. So exactly 58% of the patients in both arms would be patients who do not qualify for pembrolizumab alone.

Of course, they do break these findings down by PD-L1 subgroup, and median PFS for patients with PD-L1 50% or higher appeared to benefit from ivonescimab treatment, as well (median PFS, 11.1 vs. 8.2 months). And the unstratified hazard ratio is still 0.48, so this is similar to the benefit seen in the full population. But this underperforms what you would expect from pembrolizumab alone in these patients, which is a median of more like 10.3 months from the KEYNOTE-042 trial.

Why does this matter if we're not allowed to make cross-trial comparisons? The PD-L1 “high” subgroup of patients being less than half of the study population means that most of the patients were not receiving the standard of care we would accept in the United States. And small numbers are more frequently associated with “unusual” results, including underperformance of a control arm.

Consider, for example, that SMMT is running the HARMONi-7 trial designed to confirm the benefit seen in HARMONi-2, and this trial is enrolling only patients with high PD-L1 expression. If this study powered to answer the relevant question of pembrolizumab vs. ivonescimab shows similar results for ivonescimab, but the control arm performs as expected from historical data, then the likelihood of a significant difference goes down.

Other notable issues with respect to HARMONi-2 include the following:

  1. More patients in the pembrolizumab arm had brain metastases (17% vs. 20%); HARMONi-7 will not allow symptomatic or large CNS metastases
  2. More patients in the pembrolizumab arm were older than 65 (51% vs. 58%)
  3. Slightly more patients in the ivonescimab arm had very high PD-L1 expression (11% vs. 9%)

These are small differences, of course, but small differences can make or break trials. And some readers have commented that I've droned on so much about the questions that remain from HARMONi-2 when SMMT has never said they would pursue approval based only on those data. I fully agree.

However, these discrepancies and questions are the very reason that I will not be surprised if a properly designed study fails to recapitulate the Chinese study. It is an important risk that you cannot discount yet.

And note that there are quite a few Chinese clinical trials that failed to end up being reproduced in a manner acceptable for ex-China use. This is why we do not prescribe afatinib for gastric cancer or donafenib for liver cancer.

The Numerous Other Threads Being Developed for Ivonescimab

SMMT and Akeso are not setting their sights only on pembrolizumab's spot in NSCLC. This would be an important one to knock off the pedestal, of course, but the companies are being very aggressive in initiating pivotal trials across a variety of indications, all representing other shots on goal for the drug.

Here's a brief list of the various "major" clinical trials that have been activated:

  • HARMONi-3 and -6: Ivonescimab plus chemotherapy vs. pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy for squamous cell NSCLC
  • HARMONi-GI1: Ivonescimab plus chemotherapy for biliary tract cancer
  • SOLO-10: Ivonescimab vs. pembrolizumab for PD-L1-positive head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
  • HARMONi-BC1: Ivonescimab plus chemotherapy for triple-negative breast cancer

And others are planned or activated in settings like pancreatic cancer, colorectal cancer, and hepatocellular carcinoma.

All of this provides a semblance of balance to the questions I'm raising about the strength of the results we've seen gain a lot of hype. The companies have put many, many irons in the fire, and this positions them to find a niche for ivonescimab if the HARMONi-7 trial does not confirm the initial results. Or, if HARMONi-7 is successful, these trials give the companies a platform for very rapid expansion into other tumor areas.

Financial Overview

At the end of 2024, SMMT held $104.9 million in cash and equivalents, as well as another $307.5 million in short-term investments. Operating expenses reached $226.3 million, with R&D expenses surging from $59.5 million to $150.8 million between 2023 and 2024, underscoring the aggressive expansion of research efforts.

After interest, the net loss for the year was $221.3 million. The actual cash used for operations was $142.1 million for 2024, implying a cash runway of between 2 and 3 years. Notably, SMMT raised over $430 million via private placements for the year.

Bottom-Line Summary: Why I Remain Both Skeptical and Hopeful

The impetus for today's article was the aforementioned early exercise of warrants by the company's chief executives, which was viewed as a roaring bullish signal by the markets. Why would the man who holds 70% of SMMT's outstanding shares buy more at these levels? Because he believes they're going to go up and the market wants in on that. Seeking Alpha reported that this is likely in anticipation of an overall survival readout for HARMONi-2, which many are expecting to be positive.

There's no arguing that confidence from the CEO of a company is being demonstrated when they acquire more shares of the company. And for some, these kinds of signals are all the news you need. The main person in question here is a legendary investor, a genius behind the success of Pharmacyclics and their multi-billion-dollar success. What he touches is gold.

But when it comes to whether SMMT is a good investment at these levels, I think it is worth considering that the market is pricing in success like SMMT itself prints money already. At a market cap of around $17 billion, if SMMT were allowed to have Merck's price-to-sales ratio, they are already being priced in at having $5 billion in sales. Pembrolizumab, which has over 40 approved indications, generates sales of almost $30 billion per year. And you can mark my words that the journey for ivonescimab will be messier than a complete overthrow of pembrolizumab overnight.

In essence, the market is telling me right now that SMMT has in its hands 1/6 of the market that pembrolizumab has. It just does not resonate with reality. And while I admit that reality frequently disagrees with what I, a singular, anonymous contributor on Seeking Alpha, view as reasonable, it is important to understand why this project is far from the guarantee that the market is pricing in.

Make no mistake: ivonescimab is one of the most important emerging stories to come out of 2024 and 2025, and it could very well be a disruptive force in oncology. But to expect that it means SMMT should command buyout prices on the level of Celgene, Genentech or Shire before the company has ever sold the drug rings as highly premature.

If you are a fan of the developing story that is SMMT, I think that's justified. I would personally not endorse leveraging funds into the story at this time, not without further proof of principle. The downside risk is too high that a single marginal or negative data readout could burst the entire bubble, and the findings from HARMONi-2 on their own do not go far enough to justify this yet, not while there are important questions unanswered. Therefore, selling some of a position that one has been holding would be a justified move as well.
